token_cost.note: Estimated from word count (words * 1.35). Response-only; prompt side not counted.

efficiency.interpretation: With-skill is ~4.2x more verbose per rubric point but wins strictly on 4 of 5 criteria (correctness, completeness, structure, actionability). Clarity is the sole loss, driven entirely by verbosity — the without-skill answers read fast because they are short, not because they convey the material better.

rationale: Consistent +3 / +5 / +5 / +6 eval-level deltas across architecture mapping, execution tracing, abstraction surfacing, and change-scoping — the three skill pillars plus a cross-cutting change question. The +4.75/25 aggregate delta is identical to the dsa-expert iteration-1 run, a useful calibration. The with-skill responses consistently (a) open with a 'critical reframe' up-front (e.g., plan_exec does NOT dispatch; agentfiles has no runtime loader) that the without-skill answers bury or omit, (b) carry a fork-point enumeration that a reader can rely on, and (c) end with a 'when to stop' stop-criteria section answering concrete 'where would I add X / what breaks / what depends on Y' questions. Token overhead is real (~4.2x per response) but the target use case — onboarding to an unfamiliar repo — is a one-shot cost that produces reusable documentation, so the verbosity is appropriate. The narrowest eval-level delta (+3 on architecture-layers) is the one where the without-skill answer was already very strong on correctness (5/5) and clarity (5/5); the skill's gain there comes from completeness and actionability pitfalls, which the baseline agent would typically need a follow-up to surface.
